title: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)

lines.text: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)
Core analogies used in this lecture:

Organoid = a stem cell put in the right environment grows itself into a miniature organ — you don't sculpt it, you give it the cues and it self-assembles Matrigel = the scaffolding the cells climb on — a soup of basement-membrane proteins that gives them a 3D structure to build around Growth factor cocktail = the conductor's score — each factor tells the cells what to become, when, in what order Intestinal organoid = the original — Clevers grew a tiny crypt-villus structure in 2009 and showed

Lgr5+ stem cells could regenerate the whole gut lining in a dish Cerebral organoid = Lancaster grew mini-brains from iPSCs that recapitulate cortical layers, ventricles, even neurogenesis — at small scale, but real biology Tumor organoid for personalized medicine = take a piece of YOUR tumor, grow it as an organoid, test 100 drugs in parallel, treat you with whichever one shrinks the dish Tumor biobank = a library of patient avatars — each one a tiny biological replica of a real person's cancer,

available for experimentation Assembloids = two organoids that fuse — Pasca puts cortical + striatal together and watches neurons migrate from one into the other What they CAN'T do = no blood vessels (so size limit ~mm), no immune cells (so no inflammation studies), no aging (so most are like a mid-trimester fetus) Brain organoid ethics = if a brain organoid can fire neurons in synchrony, is it conscious?

probably not at this size — but the question is on the radar Organoid-on-chip = the engineering solution to the vasculature problem — pump fluid through, simulate blood flow, keep the interior alive Developmental biology in a dish = the cocktail reverse-engineers embryonic signals, so the cells think they're in an embryo and do what cells in an embryo do
Three core architectural elements of any working organoid system:

Stem cell source (adult tissue stem cells or pluripotent stem cells) ECM gel (Matrigel or a defined alternative) providing 3D scaffolding Growth factor cocktail recapitulating the developmental signals for the target tissue
Three core limitations of current organoids:
No vasculature — diffusion-limited size ceiling of a few millimeters No immune cells — limits modeling of inflammation and immunotherapy Immature — most resemble fetal more than adult tissue
Key historical milestones:

2007 — Clevers identifies Lgr5 as the intestinal stem cell marker 2009 — Sato and Clevers grow the first intestinal organoids from single Lgr5+ cells (Nature) 2011 — Sasai demonstrates self-organizing optic cup morphogenesis in mouse ES cells 2012 — Sasai extends optic cup work to human pluripotent cells 2013 — Takebe demonstrates iPSC-derived liver buds with vascularization upon transplant 2013 — Lancaster and Knoblich publish cerebral organoid protocol and microcephaly model 2015 — Melissa

Little publishes the first kidney organoid protocol 2016 — Multiple groups use cerebral organoids to model Zika virus infection 2017 — Pasca publishes the first cortical-subpallial assembloid 2020 — Multiple organoid systems rapidly deployed for SARS-CoV-2 research 2022 — FDA Modernization Act 2.0 explicitly permits non-animal models (including organoids) for drug development
Key researchers and their contributions:

Hans Clevers — Lgr5 identification, intestinal organoids, tumor biobanks Toshiro Sato — first intestinal organoid culture system Madeline Lancaster — cerebral organoid protocol Jürgen Knoblich — cerebral organoid biology and microcephaly modeling Yoshiki Sasai — retinal organoids and self-organizing morphogenesis Takanori Takebe — vascularized liver organ buds Melissa Little — kidney organoid protocols Ryuji Morizane — parallel kidney organoid development Sergiu Pasca — assembloids and brain

region fusion Marc van de Wetering — patient-derived tumor organoid clinical applications Don Ingber — organ-on-a-chip and microfluidic devices Josef Penninger — vascular organoids Jason Spence — lung organoids
Key organ systems with established organoid protocols:

Intestine, stomach, liver, pancreas (endocrine and exocrine), kidney, lung (proximal and distal), retina, brain (multiple regions), prostate, breast, bladder, salivary gland, thyroid, endometrium, choroid plexus, blood vessels, cardiac tissue.
Where the field is heading:

Vascularization through co-culture, chip-based perfusion, and 3D-printed vascular networks Immune cell integration to model inflammatory and infectious disease Multi-region assembloids modeling circuit-level biology Body-on-a-chip systems linking multiple organoids for systemic drug modeling Clinical-grade organoid biobanks for routine personalized medicine Transplant-ready tissue grown from patient cells for regenerative medicine Ethical frameworks for advanced cerebral organoid research as
complexity increases
